air Political Practices 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Hay 24, 1976 
Paul B. Wells 
Procopio, Cory, Hargreaves 
and Savitch 
1900 California First Bank 
Building 
530 B Street 
San Diego, California 92101 
Dear Mr. Wells: 
Your letter of May 5, 1976, to John Greenwood has been 
referred to me for a response. 
On the basis of facts stated 
in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego 
Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a "committee" 
pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes 
monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar 
year. 
As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity 
and makes no expenditures or non-monetary contributions it is not 
a "committee" as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). 
Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government 
Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not 
"contributions". 
You stated that in 1973, CIAF made no reportable 
contributions. 
In 1974, it contributed 4.4% of its income to 
committees supporting or opposing ballot measures and in 1975 
made no reportable contributions. 
Based on this prior course of 
conduct as well as the arrangement under which individual contractors 
make payments to the fund, you have concluded correctly that the 
funds received should not be characterized as "contributions". 
This informal opinion is based only on the facts stated in 
your letter and would not be applicable if CIAF changes its 
procedures and begins to engage in political activities on a 
regular basis so that the contractor has reason to know that a 
portion of his payments will be used to make contributions or 
expenditures. 
I hope this information will be of assistance. 
Sincerely, 
Natalie E. wes2~ LueAJ/ 
Staff Counsel

Thank you for your letter of June 22, 1976 requesting 
a clarification of my letter to the Fair Political Practices 
Commission of May 5, 1976, with respect to whether our client, 
San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund, makes con-
tributions to campaigns involving candidates for elective 
office. 
From the very inception of the San Diego Construction 
Industry Advancement Fund there has been a firm written policy 
that no grants will be used to in any way support or oppose 
any candidate for any political office, federal, state or 
local. 
This policy is and has been strictly adhered to and 
will continue to be in the future. 
If you should have any,other questions,

Fund - Reporting Duties Pursuant to Political 
Reform Act of 1974 
Dear lif1I'. Greenwood: 
Pursuant to your suggestion to Mr. Fiorello, a law 
clerk with our firm, this is to request an informal opinion 
from you concerning the reporting duties of our client, San 
Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund,!! (hereafter 
IICIAFH), under the Political Reform Act of 1974 (hereafter 
Uthe Act"). 
The ClAP is a non-profit trust and is exempt from 
both federal and state income tax reporting requirements. 
It WaR ~~tabli 
in 1972 as a part of the collective 
bargaining process between three employer associations, the 
Associated General Contractors, San Diego Chapter, Inc., 
("AGC"), the San Diego Building Contractors Association 
("BCA") and the Engineering and Grading Contractors Asso-
ciation, Inc$2/ 
("EGCA tI
) 
the five maior local con-
struction unions, the Laborers Union, Carpenters Union, 
l/nue to the collective bargaining process, there are 
actually two trusts, San Diego Construction Industry 
Advancement Fund and San Diego Construction Industry 
Advancement Fund (OE). 
San Diego Construction Industry 
Advanc~~ent Fund (OE) 
s funds 
upon hours 
worked by members of the 
Union. 
The 
other Fund receives monies 
upon hours worked by mem-
bers of four other basic construction industry unions. Both 
Funds are controlled by the 
trustees and are 
managed, in 
, as a single 
2/This name was later changed to Engineering and General 
Contractors Association, Inc. 


Mr. JOml C. Greenwood 
May 5, 1976 
Page 'l'Wo 
construction Teamsters Union, Cement Masons Union and 
Operating Engineers Union. 
The three employer associa-
tions bargain on behalf of most of the construction con-
tractors in San Diego County. 
tl'1der the labor agreements each contractor \-;hich 
employs members of any of the five basic construction 
unions is 
to pay a fixed sunl per man-hour ~'lorked 
to the ClAP. 
An example of the contract clause requiring 
such payments is: 
"For the purposes of protecting and 
promoting the interests and ,,,,elfars of the 
construction industry, its individual EMPLOYERS 
anel employees, the parties hereto agree to the 
establishment of a CONSTRUCTION INDUSTRY ADVANCE-
MENT FUND 'Vlhich shall comply vii th all applicable 
la'itlS and regulations 
which shall be based 
upon an EI,1PLOYER contribution of blO cents (2c) 
per hour for each hour 
for Carpenters, 
effective ~1:ay 15, 1974, and 
ing to five 
cents (5c) 
hour f'xc each hour for Carpent(~rs 
effective July I, 1974." 
(JI!laster Labor ]igreernent for San Diego County 
Covering Construction "'Jork by Carpfmters, 
June 16, 1974 -
June 15; 1977; section 50.) 
This swn \"a9 initially two cents ($0.02) per hour and is 
now generally five cents ($0.05) per hour. 
fEha payments 
are typically made on a monthly basis to the a&ninistrators 
for the various labor union health and \ilel 
, vacation 
and pension 'tru~ts and fan-larded to the ClAP by those 
admil1istrators.~1 
~/on a periodic 
is, usually monthly, each employer remits 
to each union's trust administrator 
total amount of fringe 
benefit 
by 
that union. 
For con-
venience, 
inc 
its payments to ClAF in that 
total amount. 
'l'he administrator then pays to the ClAF the 
amount due. 
The ClAF is never currently apprised of the 
identity of the employers who contribute, since those records 
remain 
trust 
trators. 
Annually, the trust 
administrators 
the 
with the names and addresses 
of all employers who made payments during the previous year. 


Mr. John C. Greenwood 
May 5, 1976 
Page Three 
The CIAF receives funds indirectly from every con-
tractor which employs members of the basic construction 
unions in San Diego County_ 
This includes not only con-
tractors resident in the county but also contractors from 
other areas of the state and the United States who engage 
in construction in this county, often on only a single 
project or other short-term basis. 
Such non-resident 
contractors are subject to the terms of the San Dieqo 
County collective bargaining agreements during their work 
in the county. 
As is evident, the number and identity of contributors 
to the ClAP and the amount of contributions may and do vary 
substantially from month to month, depending upon the amount 
of vlOrk in progress, t.he nwnber of contractors engaged in 
local construction, the severity of local weather conditions 
and the myriad other variables inherent in the industry. 
'1'he ClAF has no way of knowing from whom it will receive 
funds or the amount of those funds from month to month. 
~or 
example, in 1975, contributions ranged from a 10'# of 
$40,110.78 in March to a high of $54, 461.10 in June. 
The 
total nuw~er of contributors in 1975 exceeded 2400. 
The mana.gement of t,he ClAF is vested in its Board of 
Trustees. 
The Board is comprised of seven (7) persons t 
t'lflO 
appointed by each of the three employer associations and one 
appointed from the public at large by the other six trustees. 
None of the trustees receives any compensation for his ser-
vice on the Board. 
Other than continuing overhead (office rental, supplies, 
staff and the like), all expenditures by ClAP are the results 
of approved applications for grants. 
Grants may be requested 
by any person or organization and must be requested in writing. 
Grant applications are considered by the Board of Trustees in 
light of many factors, including the availability of funds, 
similarity to other projects funded by the ClAF, legality of 
purpose and the proposed method of its accomplishment. 
'1'11e 
single most important factor, hovlever, is how closely the 
objective of ~le grant is related to the s 
purposes of 
the ClJ\F, which are set out in Sections 1.1 through 1.4 of 
the enclosed copy of the Trust Agreement!/. 
No contributing 
!I'rho Trust Agreement 
been amended from time to time since 
this booklet ".,ras published. 
None of the amendments has any 
effect upon Sections 1.1-1.4 or this opinion request. 


Mr. John C. Greenwood 
Hay 5, 1976 
Page Four 
employer has any right to control the expenditure of any 
of the funds received by the ClAF, e.g., Section 6.3 of 
the '11rust Agreement. 
Grant applications are considered by the Board of 
Trustees at its monthly meeting and are approved or re-
jected on the basis of factors l2:xiating at that time. 
No 
general 
the granting of 
to various cate-
gories is established on a yearly or other periodic basis, 
due in grca't part to the unpredictability of the number and 
type of grant applications and the receipt of funds. 
That 
is, there is no att~npt to program on a yearly or other 
periodic basis the amounts of money or percentages of re-
ceipts vlhich will or may be granted 
use in such general 
areas as education and training, public relations, safety, 
ballot measure support or opposition, research and other 
categories for \',h1ch grants have been made in the past and 
may be sought in the future. 
The ClAP has in the past granted funels to applicants 
supporting and opposing various ballot measures affecting 
t;.te construction industry, follm'ling the procedures set out 
above. 
It mayor may not do so in the future, again follm-l-
ing the procedures set. out above. 
At no time is it possible 
to state \V'nat amounts of money or percentage of receipts, if 
any! will bE''! 
d~v(')tf!d: direct ly or indirectly; to purposes con-
cerning which reports of one kind or another may be required 
by the Act. 
For example, in 1973 the ClAF made no reportable 
contributions. 
In 1974, it contributed 4.4% of its income to 
corrnnittees supporting or opposing various ballot measures and 
in 1975 it made no such contributions. 
We have advised the ClAF that it will be a "cortUllittee" 
pursuant to sectlon B20l3(c) of the Act at such times as it 
makes contributions in the total amount of $5,000.00 or more 
in a calendar year and that it will be subject to the report-
ing requirements of such a committee. 
We have further advised the crAP that, in our opinion, 
it is not a "committee" as defined in either section 820l3{a) 
or 82l03{b). 
With respect to section 82103(b), the ClAP 
makes no contributions oUler than contributions described 
in section 820l3(c) and, consequently, it could not be an 
82013(b) 
corr~ittee. 


Hr. John C. Groam-lood 
Hay 5, 1976 
Pago Five 
With respect to section 82013(a), the CIAF does not, 
in our opinion, receive Hcontributions" as defined in the 
Act, section 820YS. 
A "contribution" is "a pa~lent ... unless 
it is clear fron the surrounding circur.lstances that it \':as 
not made for political purposes." 
On April 22, 1976, the COlTh"11ission adopted a Regulation 
with respect to contributions. 
(2 Cal.Admin. Code 18215, 
hereafter "Reg. 18215".) 
This regulation provi~es that 
"'Contribution' includes any monetary or non-
monetary payrnent ••• that is received by or made 
at behest of ..• [a]n organization fODqed or 
, t' 
. 
'1 
f 
l't' 
1 
.. 
eX1S 1ng pr1mar1 y .or po 1 1ca purposes .••• 
(Reg. 18214 (b) (4) emphasis added.) 
The enclosed Trust ,1\gree-
ment reveals that the ClAF exists for nllTIlE:;rous purpos~!5, among 
the least of which is political. 
If, among its wide array of 
grants, the CIAF occasionally cnntributes to the support or 
opposition of a ballot measure, this activity certainly does 
not constitute it as an organization existing primarily for 
political purposes. 
It!oreover I the Commission's recent regulation on contri-
butions also provides, in pertinent part: 
"Any monetary ••• pa:'iTTIlent ••• received by any ••• 
Oi,:]C1Ll.i.Zcttion ••• is <:1. contribution if t.he payment 
is 'eanlRrked t for the making of contributions 
or E;xpenZa tures. 
A payment is ' earmarked' when, 
at the time of making Uw payment, the uonor 
knm-vs or has reason to know that the payment or 
~--.--->---
funds 
th 'lrlhl.c!1 the payment viil1 be commingled 
will be used to make contributions •••• " 
(Reg. 182l4(d), emphasis added.) 
In addition, the Regulation provides: 
relevant to a determination that the 
reason to knm1 that. all or part of 
his 
will be used to make expenditures 
and contributions include but are not limited 
to the established practJce of the .•. organiza-
tion vii til res??ect to ••• contributions and any 
\.;rhen 
payment is sOlic __ • t' 
(Id., emphasis added.) 
First, pa.yments to the CIAF ctre not 
ea:rmark<~d for either political contrj.butions or expt-:nditures. 


Hr. John C. Greenwood 
Nay 5, 1976 
Page Six 
Second, the ClAP's established practice as set forth above 
shows that the contractors have no reason to know that any 
part of the funds will be used for political purposes. 
Third, 
at the time a contractor becomes bound to the collective bar-
gaining agreements and thereafter, the CIAF makes no repre-
sentations that the payments \vill be used for any particular 
purposes, political or othe~dse. Finally I the contractors I 
payments are not solicited but arc required by labor ue]ree-
menta. 
It aeems inarguable that the individual contractors 
.. vhich make payment~; to the fund administrators for the ClAP 
do not make then "for political purposes". 
The contractors 
make the payments because they are required to do so by a 
collective bargaining agreement with which they Dust comply 
in order to engage in their business. 
They are subject to 
civil liability for failure to make the paynents. 
'l'hey 
have no choice in the matter nor have they any right to 
direct or control the disposition of the funds received by 
the CIll.F.'. 
In.deed, they have no knowledge of how the funds 
are specifically expended, absent inquiry after the fact. 
The payments are in no \vay similar to "duesl! paid by r.lcmb(!r.'s 
of voluntary organizations, as those mernlx~rs may decide not 
to pay their dues or to withdra\v from the organization. 'Vlith-
out injuring their businesses or incurring civil liability. 
The payments are far more similar to IldUf;S" paid by members 
of labor unions as a conaition to retaining their jobs. 
In light of the circumstances set out above, it seems 
clear that the CL'\F is not il ItcO'lmni·ttee" as defined in 
section 82103(a) of the Act and is not required to file a 
Committe(~ Cfu"l1paign Statement, form 420. 
The ClAP has no desire to withhold any information con-
cerning itself, its activities or its sources of funds. 
Its 
books and records are open to any person or agency vii th a 
legitimate interest in them. 
It has filed and will continue 
to file all reports required of a major donor when it comes 
within that category_ 
On the other hand, the proper preparation of a form 420 
Schedule 1\ (List of Contributor::; ,·dth "AI:1ount Received" and 
"Cumulati ve Amount Ii) would be almost if not cO!!lpletely impos-
sible and would, in any event, be a staggering burden. 
As 
stated 
f there were som(:~ 2400 
e.mployers who 
made payments to the ClAP through the various trust admini-
strators during 1975. 
Hany made paymonts each month, others 
undoubtedly reade as few as one payment. 


John C. Greenwood 
May 5, 1976 
Page Seven 
The ClAP has no knowledge of the date or dates upon 
which a particular employer makes a payment which ulti-
mately flows to the crAP. 
It is no doubt the case that 
some employer will make its first payment in November. If 
the ClAP had contributed to a ballot measure committee in 
the preceding April, no one could argue that the employer 
making the November pa:,{ment had made a "contribution II. 
While we believe it is clear that the ClAP is not a 
section 82013(a) cormnittee, in light of the very heavy 
penalties both criminal and civil which may be imposed for 
violation of the Act, it seems prudent to seek your con-
currence in this matter. 
I will certainly appreciate your attention to this 
important matter and vlill be happy to provide any further 
information which you may desire.

sections.question: What are the reporting duties of the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether CIAF constitutes a 'committee' requiring disclosure and reporting?
sections.conclusion: On the basis of facts stated in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a 'committee' pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar year. As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity and makes no expenditures or non-monetary contributions it is not a 'committee' as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not 'contributions'. Based on the prior course of conduct and the arrangement under which individual contractors make payments to the fund, the funds received should not be characterized as 'contributions'.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund, a non-profit trust established through collective bargaining that receives per-hour contributions from construction contractors and makes grants to various causes including some ballot measure campaigns, qualifies as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act of 1974 requiring disclosure and reporting.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: CIAF will be a committee only if it makes monetary contributions of $5,000 or more in a calendar year under Section 82013(c). The funds CIAF receives are not 'contributions' under the Act because they are mandatory per-hour payments under collective bargaining agreements rather than voluntary political contributions, and CIAF is not a committee under sections 82013(a) or (b) as long as it engages in no independent campaign activity.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains FPPC response letter dated May 24, 1976, plus supporting correspondence from the requesting law firm. The response is clear with explicit Q/A structure. Includes follow-up correspondence clarifying candidate support policy. Minor OCR issues but core content extracted reliably.

QUESTION: What are the reporting duties of the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether CIAF constitutes a 'committee' requiring disclosure and reporting?

CONCLUSION: On the basis of facts stated in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a 'committee' pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar year. As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity and makes no expenditures or non-monetary contributions it is not a 'committee' as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not 'contributions'. Based on the prior course of conduct and the arrangement under which individual contractors make payments to the fund, the funds received should not be characterized as 'contributions'.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF), a non-profit trust receiving mandatory per-hour contributions from construction contractors under collective bargaining agreements, will qualify as a 'committee' only if it makes monetary contributions totaling $5,000 or more in a calendar year, and that the funds it receives are not 'contributions' under the Political Reform Act.
